Work Text:
The Great Hall was warm and loud, a fortress against the storm hammering the enchanted ceiling. The air smelled of toasted bread and oatmeal. Remus Lupin sat at the Gryffindor table, feeling surprisingly solid. It was two weeks until the moon—that golden window where the scars were faded silver and the bone-deep ache hadn't yet returned. He felt capable. He felt hungry.

He stacked a third pancake onto his plate.

"So," Sirius Black was saying, gesturing with a piece of toast, "I told Flitwick that the collision of the two desks was a commentary on the socio-economic struggles of goblin rebellion architecture."

"He was probably crying because you singed his eyebrows, Padfoot," James Potter replied.

But James’s voice lacked its usual boom. He was hunched over his plate, his face a pale, waxy shade under the messy black hair. He wasn't eating. He was just staring at a sausage as if it were a poisonous beetle.

"I didn't singe them," Sirius argued. "They just... receded. In fear."

Peter Pettigrew giggled, spraying crumbs across the table.

Remus watched James. He looked wrong. There was a sheen of sweat on James's forehead that had nothing to do with the heat of the room.

"Prongs?" Remus asked quietly. "You alright?"

James blinked, looking up slowly. "Yeah," he said, his voice rough. He rubbed a hand over his face. "Actually... no. I've been feeling a bit under the weather. Since last night."

"You should go to Pomfrey," Remus said immediately.

"Nah," James waved a hand. He swallowed hard, his throat clicking. "Just need to... get some air."

The smell of the sausages seemed to suddenly saturate the space between them. James’s face shifted from pale to a alarming shade of green.

"James?" Sirius leaned in.

James clapped a hand over his mouth. He doubled over.

A stream of vomit exploded across the golden platters of sausages and bacon. Splatter hit the goblets and dripped onto the tablecloth. The smell of bile cut through the warm breakfast aromas like a knife.

For a second, nobody moved.

Peter let out a high-pitched squeak and scrambled backward, nearly tumbling off the bench. Sirius shot upright, his knees catching the underside of the table and jostling it, which sent a jug of pumpkin juice tipping over. Orange liquid spread toward the edge, mingling with the mess.

"Bloody hell, Prongs!" Sirius yelped, pinching his nose. "That was visceral!"

James gasped, spitting onto the mess. He looked up, eyes watering, utterly humiliated. "Told... you..."

He swayed dangerously.

"Right, that's it," Remus said, sliding out from the bench and grabbing James's arm. "Sirius, grab his other arm. Hospital Wing. Now."

"This is why I stick to toast," Sirius muttered, though he was already gripping James's shoulder, supporting the weight with surprising gentleness. "Come on, you disaster. Let's get you to bed."

"I'm fine," James mumbled weakly, wiping his mouth on his sleeve. "Just... a bug."

"Yeah, a bug that just murdered the Gryffindor breakfast," Sirius said, steering him toward the doors. "Madam Pomfrey is going to love this."

As they moved away, a sharp swish echoed behind them. Remus glanced back. Professor McGonagall had descended from the high table, her lips pressed into a thin line. With a brusque wave of her wand, she vanished the mess in an instant. A twist of her wrist replaced the soiled tablecloth with a fresh, white one, and a new platter of sausages popped into existence, steaming and innocent, as if nothing had ever happened.

Remus turned back to the cool air of the entrance hall. It wasn't fatal, but it was messy enough to ensure James wouldn't be leaving the Hospital Wing for a week. Madame Pomfrey didn't mess around with stomach bugs.

Remus felt a strange, sharp pang of guilt. James was miserable. But in the back of his mind, a quiet thought took root: He's gone.

Sirius would be pacing the common room within the hour, climbing the walls with boredom and worry. He would need a distraction. And Remus, for the first time in weeks, felt strong enough to be one.

The Hospital Wing smelled of liniment and starch. Madam Pomfrey took one look at James’s greenish pallor and ushered him into a curtained bed with the efficiency of a field medic.

"Stomach flu," she diagnosed, waving her wand over a glass of potion that turned a violent shade of purple. "Drink this. And don't let him leave that bed for three days, minimum."

James swallowed the potion with a grimace and collapsed back against the pillows, looking pathetic.

"Try not to die, Prongs," Sirius said, backing away as Pomfrey shooed them toward the door. "We'll bring you homework."

"Don't you dare," James mumbled, already pulling the sheets up.

They sprinted down the marble staircase, taking two steps at a time. The corridors were emptying; the bell was due to ring any second.

They slid through the door of the Transfiguration classroom just as the clock on the wall began to chime. Professor McGonagall was writing on the blackboard, her back straight as a rod. She didn't turn around, but her chalk paused for a fraction of a second as they tiptoed to their usual seats in the back row.

Remus sat down, breathless. A second later, Sirius dropped into the chair beside him.

The chair on the other side of Sirius remained empty. James’s usually chaotic desk was clear—no half-finished essay, no doodles of Snitches, no stack of Chocolate Frog cards.

McGonagall turned around, her spectacles glinting. She scanned the room, her gaze flickering over the empty seat.

"Mr. Black," she said. "I see Mr. Potter has decided to spare my classroom the contents of his stomach."

She turned back to the board, tapping it with her wand. The words HUMAN TO ANIMAGUS: THE PHYSIOLOGY OF TRANSFORMATION appeared in elegant, swirling script.

"Open your books to page 394," she commanded.

The rest of the day seemed to drag. Without James to whisper jokes across the History of Magic aisle or pass illicit notes during Charms, the castle felt strangely large and echoing. The other students went about their business, but the gap at the Gryffindor table was impossible to ignore.

By the time the final bell rang, the gray light of afternoon was already fading into a dreary twilight. Remus and Sirius walked back to the tower in a comfortable silence, though Sirius kept tapping his wand against his leg, a rhythmic, impatient beat.

The common room was a welcome relief of warmth and noise. The fire was roaring in the grate, casting flickering orange shadows over the worn armchairs. It was crowded, students sprawled on the rug finishing essays or huddled in corners gossiping.

Remus claimed his usual seat near the fire, sinking into the worn velvet. He opened The Dark Forces: A Guide to Self-Protection, grateful for the distraction. The words on the page blurred slightly—he was tired, but it was a normal tiredness, not the bone-crushing exhaustion of the week before.

Sirius dropped into the chair opposite him, throwing his legs over the armrest. He pulled out a roll of parchment and a quill, but instead of writing, he spent ten minutes trying to see if he could balance the inkwell on the end of his nose.

"It's going to tip," Remus said, not looking up from his chapter on counter-curses. "And you're going to stain the rug, and Filch will make you scrub it with a toothbrush."

"You have no sense of adventure, Moony," Sirius mumbled, the inkwell wobbling precariously. "It's about balance. Focus. Inner peace."

"It's about making a mess."

The portrait hole swung open with a creak, letting in a draft of cooler air. Peter Pettigrew scrambled through, looking flushed and breathless. He craned his neck, spotted them by the fire, and hurried over, dodging a pair of first-years playing Wizard's Chess.

"Well?" Sirius sat up so fast the inkwell slid off his nose and landed safely in his lap with a soft thud. "Is he still alive? Did he survive the Great Sausage Massacre?"

Peter collapsed onto the rug between their chairs, wiping sweat from his forehead. "He’s asleep. Pomfrey gave him a Sleeping Draught."

"Poor Prongs," Sirius said, though a grin was tugging at the corner of his mouth. "Finally, some peace and quiet."

"He looks awful," Peter added, pulling a squashed bag of Bertie Bott's Every Flavor Beans from his pocket. "Pale as a sheet. She says he's severely dehydrated. She wouldn't let me stay—she said he needs to rest. He won't be up for dinner, and she said she’s keeping him overnight. Maybe two days."

"Two days?" Sirius raised his eyebrows, tossing a bean into his mouth and grimacing—boiled cabbage flavor. "For a stomach bug? She just likes the drama of it all."

"She said he needs to be monitored," Peter mumbled, chewing rapidly. "She wasn't happy when I tried to wake him up to say hi. She practically shoved me out the door."

Dinner had been uneventful. The food was bland, the conversation sparse, and they finished quickly. They headed up to the dormitory to change.

The room was filled with the usual sounds of the evening—buckles clinking, robes hitting the floor. Sirius was stripping off his clothes with aggressive, jerky movements. Peter had turned his back to the room, changing with frantic modesty.

Sirius turned, spotted him, and let out a short, sharp, barking laugh.

"Oi, Wormtail," Sirius crowed, leaning against his bedpost with a predatory grin. "Where's your knob? Did you forget to do that bit last time you transformed back into a human?"

Remus flinched. "Keep your voice down," he hissed, crossing the room. "Do you want the whole school to hear?"

"Hear what Moony? About Peter's teeny weenie? It's not news mate."

Peter flushed a deep, miserable red, snatching his pajama bottoms and yanking them up with trembling hands. "Oh, very funny," he muttered, his voice high and tight. "I haven't heard that one a thousand times."

He thrust his hand up, extending two fingers in a rude gesture right under Sirius's nose. Then, without waiting for a reaction, he turned on his heel and marched to his bed, yanking the curtains shut with a violent snap.

Remus turned toward his own four-poster and reached for the hangings, but Sirius's voice cut through the din behind him.

"Moony." He sat cross-legged on his mattress, the battered chessboard open in front of him. "James is sick. Fancy sitting in? I might actually win one tonight."

Remus came back and settled at the foot of the bed. "I'm not very good."

"I know," Sirius said with a laugh. "Pick a color."

"Black, I suppose."

"Excellent. Pawn to E4." The white stone soldier marched forward and stopped on its square.

"Pawn to E5." The black pawn stepped out to meet it, a mirror of aggression, and Sirius drummed his fingers against his knee in a restless rhythm that matched the game. "Slow tonight."

"It's been a long day."

"Dull, you mean." Sirius leaned forward, chin in hand. "Knight to F3." The white knight trotted out with a heavy clatter, carving its path through the ranks. The conversation wandered along with the pieces—the sausages James had thrown up over at breakfast, the heroic manner in which he'd done it, the relative merits of peace versus noise—and Remus answered in the half-distracted way of a man losing slowly and not minding much, until Sirius looked up from the board and didn't look back down again.

"Whatever happened with you and Cordelia Flint?" he asked. "Before she ran off to Marcus Avery. I was sure you were in there, mate, and then—gone. Was she a good snog at least?"

The heat crawled up Remus's neck. "Sirius—"

"How far did you get?"

"Not far." He kept his eyes on the polished wood of the bedframe. "I got my hand up her shirt. That was it."

"That was it?" Sirius's eyebrows climbed. "She didn't—"

"She pulled away. She saw the scars. All the ones down my side." He gestured vaguely at his own face, his voice dropping. "It's hard enough talking to girls without looking like I'm about to fall over. I'm peaky three weeks out of four. Hardly an attraction."

The mockery went out of Sirius's expression. "That's the whole catalogue, then. One hand. One shirt."

"Keep your voice down."

"Bishop takes pawn." The white bishop strode forward and cracked its crosier against the black pawn, sweeping the wreckage to the edge of the board. "That's genuinely tragic, Moony, and I'm not being dramatic."

"You're always being dramatic."

"Not about this. This I'm horrified about, properly, on your behalf." He pretended to study the board while his fingers kept up their restless drumming. "Sixteen years old and you've had one tit in your hand. That's the entire history."

"Some of us have other things to worry about. Such as not eating people once a month."

"Don't."

"Don't what?"

"Make it a joke so we stop talking about it." Sirius slid his rook out. "Rook to E1. I'm serious."

"You're Sirius, lovely to—"

"I will smother you with this pillow."

Remus moved a knight to nowhere in particular, and Sirius let the game drift, leaning back against the headboard with his eyes off the board entirely.

"Fawley," he said.

"What about her?"

"Caroline Fawley. Last Hogsmeade weekend." He said it the way he said everything that cost him nothing. "Three Broomsticks first, then the Hog's Head after. There's a back room. She used her mouth."

Remus's hand stalled over his queen. "Did she."

"That's my whole point, see." Sirius sat forward, and the grey eyes sharpened. "I have been touched by another human being. Hands, mouths, the lot. And I'm telling you it is not the same. It isn't remotely the same as doing it yourself. It's like—" He cast about for it. "You know how chocolate tastes after a transformation? After you've been torn apart and put back together and Pomfrey hands you that first square."

Remus said nothing. He knew exactly how it tasted.

"Like that. But everywhere, all at once." Sirius turned a captured pawn between his fingers. "When she went down I thought I'd pass out. Not because she was any good—God love her, she caught me with her teeth twice and I nearly went through the ceiling—but because it was somebody else. Someone else's warmth. Someone else's timing, that you can't predict. You can't do that with your own hand, Moony. Your cock knows what your hand's about to do. There's no surprise in it."

"Bishop to B4." The piece settled with a faintly disapproving look.

"You're ignoring me."

"I'm playing chess."

"You're hiding behind chess." He set the pawn down. "I know what it's like to have someone else's mouth on me. James does. Peter probably does—Mary MacDonald's been giving him a very strange look lately. You don't. And it's not because you're ugly, or boring, or whatever miserable thing you've decided about yourself. It's because one girl got spooked and you built a wall around the whole subject and moved in behind it."

Remus prodded a pawn forward, harder than the move required. "Pawn to C6."

"Defense," Sirius said. "Always defense."

The fire downstairs must have been stoked; warmth pushed up through the floorboards. Peter's snoring had thickened into a steady whistling drone two beds over.

"The point is you're missing something." Sirius's voice had dropped now, gone quieter and rougher. "Not technique. Not practice. Just the feeling. What it's like when the hand isn't yours. And because of one bad afternoon you've decided to opt out. For good, apparently."

"Not for good. Just until—" Remus stopped.

"Until what? Until the scars heal? They don't, mate, that's rather the nature of them." Sirius gestured at him with the captured pawn. "You could wait the rest of your life. And while you wait, every other person your age is finding out what it feels like to be touched, and you're up here folding your jumpers and losing at chess and seeing to yourself behind the curtains like a monk."

"A lovely portrait. Thank you."

"A deeply reassuring way to begin."

"For what?"

"For somebody else's hand." Sirius held his gaze, and his whole posture had gone carefully, studiously loose—the kind of loose that took work. "You've never been touched and it's eating you alive, and I'm sitting right here, and it wouldn't have to be a thing. A hand. Five minutes. You shut your eyes and you find out what it feels like when the grip isn't your own, and then it's done and we finish the game."

The dormitory seemed very quiet.

"You're having me on," Remus said.

"You are. This is the setup. This is the whole long con." He sat back, his jaw tightening. "Here's how it goes. I say yes, and you'll be shouting Moony said yes to a wank off a bloke until we're both forty. Or I say no, and I'm a coward, and you'll bring it up every time a girl so much as looks at me. Either way I lose. I know how you operate, Sirius."

Sirius didn't smile. He didn't move.

"Name a prank," he said quietly, "where I had to sit here first and tell you about Caroline Fawley's mouth on my cock. Name one time I've put that kind of work into a joke."

Remus opened his mouth. Closed it again.

"The Snape map business took you three days."

"That was logistics. This is me telling you something true about myself, and I don't spend that on a gag." The quiet in his voice wasn't the theatrical sort he put on for effect. It was the real thing, and that was what made Remus go still. "I've been where you are. Not the scars—the other part. The part where you're certain you're the only person alive who hasn't worked this out yet, and it's lonely as hell, and you don't have to stay there just because you're too proud to let a mate help."

Remus's face burned, and not only from embarrassment now. Something pressed at the back of his throat and made it hard to swallow.

"People don't do this," he said.

"People do all sorts of things they don't talk about."

"Blokes don't—"

"Yeah, they do," Sirius said simply. "They just lie about it after. Same as everything."

The sounds of the tower pressed in—the wind, Peter, a distant laugh from the common room below—and Remus became aware of his own heartbeat, thick and ridiculous against his ribs.

"And it wouldn't mean anything," he heard himself say. Flat. Testing.

"Not a thing. One mate doing another a favor."

A laugh escaped him in spite of everything. "That's the worst sentence you've ever spoken."

"It's the best sentence I've ever spoken and you know it."

The laugh died. The quiet came back. Remus looked down at the board, at his king standing alone in a field of wreckage, one move from checkmate. His hand found the hangings. He could pull them open, swing his legs off the bed, cross to his own four-poster, and in the dark let the whole evening dissolve into something they never spoke of again. His fingers tightened on the velvet.

He drew it shut.

"Who would know?" Sirius said.

"You would. I would."

"And?"

"And that's enough."

"Enough to feel strange about it. You feel strange about everything, Moony. Second helpings at dinner. Using the prefects' bath. You felt strange about us turning ourselves into animals for you. It's your resting state. It doesn't mean a thing."

Remus said nothing.

"Muffliato."

The charm prickled over his skin, and the world emptied of sound—Peter's snoring gone, the wind gone, the laughter from below gone, until there was nothing left but the rush of his own blood. Sirius settled back against the headboard and waited.

Remus searched his face for the tell. The twitch at the corner of the mouth that came before every prank, the brightness that meant Sirius was already composing the version he'd tell James. He looked, and kept looking, and there was nothing there but grey eyes holding steady on his.

His hands lay flat on his thighs. The flannel was warm under his palms, and his heartbeat filled the silence, absurd and enormous. He found the slit in his pajamas and worked himself free.

He was already half-hard—had been, he realized with a slow crawl of shame, for some time. He sat there with himself out through the gap in the flannel and fixed his eyes on the pattern of the duvet, certain that any second the silence would split open and Sirius would howl and the whole tower would learn what Moony had done.

The silence held.

He reached down and took hold of Remus's cock.

The hand was warm. Drier than his own. Bigger, somehow, though that made no sense—they were the same age, near enough the same size. It just felt different because it was his, because Remus hadn't known where it was going to land or how it would close around him, and the not-knowing sent a jolt up his spine that nearly bent him double.

Sirius felt it. Of course he did. The corner of his mouth pulled, but he kept his eyes down, businesslike.

"You know this is harder on me, really," he said. "You just have to lie there. I'm the one who has to touch another bloke's cock for the good of the cause. Real sacrifice, this. Practically Gryffindor of me."

A noise came out of Remus that wasn't quite a laugh. "Shut up."

This was someone else's wrist, someone else's pace, someone who watched his face and adjusted to it, who tightened his grip at the catch of breath and slowed when Remus's hips gave that involuntary twitch off the mattress. Sirius knew what he was doing. The thought drifted up through the fog and Remus was too far gone to examine it.

Remus's hands had found the duvet and fisted into it. His breath came short and ragged through his teeth. The heat was building low and fast, gathering at the base of his spine, and he heard himself make a sound he'd have died of shame over an hour ago.

I. THE FIRST ONES — ORIGIN AND NATURE
What They Are


The First Ones are an ancient people from the deep past — from before the world was
finished.
In the early ages, reality had not yet hardened into the rigid forms it now wears:
- Matter was less committed to being any particular thing
- Time flowed unevenly
- Stone remembered being water
The First Ones lived within that softer reality and were native to it. Their bodies and minds
were shaped to it as a fish is shaped to water. To them, moving a mountain or parting an
ocean was not a miracle — it was simply living in a world that still bent.
They were not gods, though they would have looked like gods to anyone watching. They
were a people — with culture, language, conflict, and history — who belonged to a reality that
no longer exists.
The Long Age Before Humans
For an immeasurable stretch of time, the world belonged to the First Ones alone.
They built cities of light and stone in places now buried beneath continents. They wrote in
languages that left no marks because their writing was carved into the texture of reality itself
— a mountain shaped to spell a name, a river whose course was a poem. They had art,
philosophy, music, mathematics. They argued. They mourned. They invented things and
forgot them again.
What they did not have, and could not imagine, was cruelty — violence whose purpose is
degradation. Pain inflicted not to change a thing but to diminish it. To make a being lesser
while keeping it alive. To own.
This blindness is important. The First Ones were not gentle by choice; they were gentle by
structural incapacity. And the absence of cruelty in their imagination did not mean an
absence of harm. A people who cannot conceive of degradation cannot detect themselves
doing degrading things.
The Slow Cooling


The world did not stay unsettled. Reality was hardening. Stone was becoming just stone.
Water was becoming just water. The give was going out of things.
With each long age, the world grew more rigid, and the First Ones found their work harder.
What had once cost a thought now cost a day. What had cost a day now cost a year.
They knew they were a people in slow decline. Most chose to let themselves dissolve back
into the world — the returning — considered a dignified end. Only a stubborn handful
refused to return.
These were the ones still alive when humans came.
Natural Form
In their natural form, First Ones look like a coherent species — beautiful, strange, and
unmistakably not human:
Feature
Description
**Height**
5'8" to 6'2", slim but strong
**Build**
Longer limbs than human, slightly elongated fingers, longer neck
**Ears**

**Eyes**

**Skin**

**Hair**

**Features**

The house-elf form that wizards know is not the natural form. It is an imposed form — the
chains themselves hold the First One in a small, deferential body, continuously, in real time.
The shape was chosen by those who forged the chains. It is not what the First Ones are. It is
what was done to them.
II. THE ARRIVAL OF HUMANS AND THE GREAT
FADING


By the time humans appeared, the First Ones were already a dying people. Still
extraordinarily powerful by any human standard, but each act cost them more than the last.
They were old gods running out of room to be gods in.
Humans, when the First Ones first encountered them, were almost beneath notice. Small,
short-lived, fragile — a species that could not reshape the world but could only endure it.
Then one of those holdouts — the First Mother, whose true name has been forgotten —
discovered that humans were warm in a way the cooling world was not. And when a First One
pressed close to that warmth, they stopped fading.
Of the First Bonding
How the First Mother came to know this, the First Ones do not agree. The story has been told
for so long that the versions no longer reconcile.
Some say she found a man dying in the snow, alone and far from his people, and she lay
down beside him to keep him warm — because warmth was a kindness, and she had not yet
known what warmth could do. He should have died. He did not. And in the morning, when she
rose, the fading that had haunted her for ages had quieted.
Others say it was a woman, not a man, and that the First Mother had been watching her for
years. When the mortal woman was wounded, the First Mother poured herself into the wound
to close it, and the pouring went deeper than she intended.
Others say the first bonding killed the mortal. That the First Mother, not knowing what she was
doing, took too much, or gave too much, or both — and the mortal died in her arms within
days, and she was left holding the first scar-echo, and from the scar-echo she finally
understood what could have been done differently.
What is agreed: the First Mother lived. The fading stopped. She told the other holdouts, and
a few tried what she had done. Some succeeded. Some did not. The mortals who survived
became the first Vhor'keth.
III. THE BOND — VHOR'KETH AND VHOR'KETHA


Mortal-Radiance
The thing the First Ones call warmth is not heat, not emotion, not life-force. It is the radiance
of finitude — the signature emitted, continuously, by a consciousness that knows at every
level of its being that it is running out of time.
Mortality is not just a fact about humans. It is a quality their existence emits, simply by being
mortal and being alive.
Implications:
- The bond works only with beings that emit mortal-radiance. Humans do, abundantly.
Animals do, but weakly.
- Vhor'kethi emit reduced radiance — they are part First One, and their mortality is less
absolute. A First One cannot bond with a Vhor'kethi. There is not enough anchor.
- Magical creatures with extended lifespans emit weaker radiance.
- Magical constructs, undead, and beings without proper mortality emit nothing. A First One
cannot bond with a ghost, a wraith, or an inferius.
- A slow death produces a long, intensifying crescendo of mortal-radiance. The scar-echo that
forms after is smooth. A sudden death cuts the radiance off mid-emission. The scar-echo that
forms is jagged. Sudden deaths leave worse wounds.
The bond does not need love. It needs only the radiance. The radiance is not consensual to
give — it emits whether the mortal wishes it to or not. This is the coercive substrate
underneath every bond ever made. The First Ones do not seem to find this troubling. It is not
clear that they can.
Vhor'keth
The human chosen by a First One. Translates as "the Chosen," or more literally, "the one
who is carried."
The bond is not a contract or obligation — the human is not made to perform any duty. The
First One chooses freely; the human, in the moment of bonding, often does not understand
what is being chosen. Most Vhor'keth, once they understand, choose to love back. Many love
back even before they understand. Some do neither. The bond holds regardless.


Vhor'ketha
The bonding itself. The act of becoming one current.
Literally, "the pouring-through" — the dissolution of the boundary that makes two beings
separate. In the Old Tongue, the sacred and the sexual were never distinguished, because to
the First Ones they were the same thing: the willing dissolution of self into another.
It is permanent. Vhor'ketha cannot be undone, refused after the fact, or transferred. Once
two beings have poured through each other, they are part of each other for as long as the
First One continues to exist.
Vhor'ketha can only be initiated by a First One. The pouring-through requires the
unsettled-reality-native body to dissolve its own boundary. Two Vhor'kethi cannot bond with
each other. Two humans cannot bond at all. The bond was, and remains, a thing only First
Ones could ever do.
What Bonding Does
For the First One:
- The human's mortal-radiance anchors them. They stop fading
- They can reshape their form at will, including taking human form
- They become obsessively protective of the human
- Their effective lifespan extends to functional immortality
For the human:
- Over the lifetime of the bond, the human is changed at the level of the body — gradual,
deep, and heritable
- The human's lifespan can be extended considerably if the First One chooses
- The human may gain modest reflections of the First One's capacities — heightened
resilience, sharper senses, longer wakefulness — but never the full reality-bending
When the Human Dies


The First One does not die. The bond leaves a permanent mark — a scar-echo — that
becomes part of the First One forever. The grief layers but does not fade. The love becomes a
thing carried, smoothed over time but never gone.
The death of a Vhor'keth does not immediately restart the fading. The scar-echo carries
residual stabilization for a time — months, sometimes years. This is the Keth'vael, the
echo-time, the grace period in which a First One can mourn before being forced to choose
whether to bond again.
The First One can bond again. Each new bond is real and whole, but each one is also loved
through the accumulated weight of every prior bond. A First One who has lost many Vhor'keth
becomes a richer and more careful lover with each one — but also more fragile.
IV. THE FALL — THE BETRAYAL AND THE CHAINS
The Vhor'kethi
The children of First One and Vhor'keth bonds. Part human, part First One. Born
appearing human but carrying the old blood. Powers beyond ordinary wizards, longer
lifespans, the capacity to walk between worlds and speak with all creatures.
The first Vhor'kethi were rare and revered. But as more bondings occurred, more Vhor'kethi
were born, and the early generations grew into a population of their own — a people without
precedent, with no elders of their own kind to teach them how to be what they were.
The Wound of the Almost
The first Vhor'kethi were born into a world that had no place for them.
They were not First Ones. They could feel the world's hardness in a way their First One
parents could not — could touch the edge of their parents' power, taste it, almost reach it, and
never quite hold it. They were the children of beings who could remake mountains, and they
themselves could light fires with a thought, mend bones, sometimes speak to animals.
Wonders, by mortal standards. Crumbs, by the standard of what they had grown up watching.


They were also not human. They aged more slowly than their mortal parents. They watched
their Vhor'keth parent grow old and die while their First One parent remained. They watched
their mortal grandparents die. Their mortal cousins. Everyone they loved on the human side
dissolved away, generation by generation.
They were, in their own estimation, almost. Almost as powerful as their First One parents.
Almost as warm as their mortal parents. Almost capable of the bond that had made them.
Almost, almost, almost — and never enough.
The Rebellion
Resentment grew across generations. Not all Vhor'kethi felt it. Many made peace with what
they were. But a faction emerged who came to see their First One parents as a problem. Not
as monsters. As injustices.
The argument: the First Ones held a kind of being the Vhor'kethi could not access and could
not earn. The First Ones would outlive them. The First Ones would mourn them, eventually,
as scars among many scars, and go on.
The logic: if the First Ones could be contained — made to do the work of reshaping at the
direction of those who could no longer do it themselves — then the power could be put to use
by those who had built civilizations in the way the First Ones never had.
The First Ones, in their understanding, were not being enslaved — they were being put to
use. Their power, otherwise wasted on the slow returning, would now serve a civilization that
could actually carry it forward.
Many of them also simply hated their parents. Hated them for being able to carry losses the
Vhor'kethi could not. Hated them for living past every human they had loved. Hated them for
the almost the Vhor'kethi could never close.
Cruelty, the concept the First Ones could not imagine, came easily to their half-mortal
children. The Vhor'kethi had inherited it from their human side — the human capacity to want
to diminish a thing, to make it smaller while keeping it alive, because diminishing it makes you
feel larger.
The Khoz'vhar — The Unshielding


The chains did not arrive by intuition. They were the product of research — a long, deliberate
program by the rebellion's architects to map the mechanism of the bond from the outside.
This program is remembered, when it is remembered at all, as the Khoz'vhar — the
unshielding, the inverse of Vorr'khoz, the protective field. Where Vorr'khoz placed a shield
around a beloved, Khoz'vhar opened a being who should never have been opened, and
looked inside.
The Khoz'vhar required First Ones to study. The rebellion took them. Early holdouts, isolated
elders, and (in the worst of the fragments) bonded First Ones whose Vhor'keth had been killed
first to make the subject more compliant. They were studied for centuries. The
pouring-through was mapped from the outside in.
What was done to the First Ones in the Khoz'vhar is not in any surviving record. The
First Ones who remember do not speak of it. When First Ones today speak of the Khoz'vhar,
they use the same tone humans use for ancestral atrocities the words have not been invented
for.
The Keth'vorr — The Silence Weave
The chains the Khoz'vhar produced do not suppress First One power. They redirect it.
A First One's reality-bending energy still flows. It is simply siphoned and converted into a
narrow band of domestic and protective magic: mending, cleaning, food preparation,
apparition-like popping, the weaving of household wards. The First One can still act, but only
within the channels the binding allows.
This is why a house-elf can apparate through powerful wards that defy human wizards,
can mend an heirloom with a touch, can produce a feast from nothing — and yet
appears utterly powerless in combat. The power is the same. The channel is narrow.
The chains also enforce a body. Continuously, in real time, the Keth'vorr imposes the small,
deferential, large-eyed form wizards know as the house-elf. The shape was chosen
deliberately:
- It makes the First One visibly lesser
- It disguises what the First One actually is
- It prevents physical resistance


- It is, in its own quiet way, a brand
The shape is not the First One. It is held on them. The moment the chains break on a
given First One, their natural form returns — not gradually, not painfully, but immediately and
completely.
The Descendant-Resonance Lock
The chains are bound to a second mechanism, deeper than the redirection. If a First One
forms violent intent against any being who carries the magic-trait — any descendant, however
distant — they experience an unbearable feedback echo of their own children's pain and fear.
The lock keys not to "wizard" but to the carrier-gene signature itself:
- A First One can in principle harm a being with no carrier genes — but this is functionally
never available, because the trait has diffused into the general human population over
millennia. Most Muggles carry the dormant trait.
- A First One cannot reliably tell, by looking, who they can hurt. The chains punish the attempt,
not the success.
- The architects considered this a feature: the lock is self-enforcing through fear.
Because all wizards descend from First One bonds, all wizards are family — and all of
them are inside the lock. The First Ones could not fight back because fighting back meant
hurting their own descendants. They were not conquered. They were trapped by their own
love.
Why the Line Went Dormant
A chained First One cannot perform Vhor'ketha. The Keth'vorr will not permit the
pouring-through. The architects specifically designed for this: they had been born of bonds
themselves, they understood the power of Vhor'kethi, and they did not want any more of their
kind walking the world.
As long as the chains hold on the population of surviving First Ones, no new bonds form, and
no new Vhor'kethi are born. The line went dormant a thousand years ago not because of any
failure of the trait, but because the conditions for bonding ceased to exist.


If a First One were freed of the chains and chose to bond with a human, a new
Vhor'kethi could be born within a generation. The dormant line is a reachable state. Not a
closed door.
V. THE ORIGIN OF WIZARDS — GENETICS
When a First One bonds with a human, something passes between them. Over the lifetime of
the bond, the human is changed at the level of the body, and that change becomes heritable.
This is what wizards call magic, and what Muggles call impossible. It is, mechanically, an
inherited trait — a set of carrier genes that travel together and behave the way carrier traits
behave in any inheritance.
Inheritance Patterns
Parent Configuration

Two carriers (non-magical)
Can produce a magical child (Muggle-born)

The Pureblood Lie
Purebloods believe their bloodlines make their magic stronger. They do not. The magic-trait
is either expressed or it isn't — having the genes "more times over" does nothing to amplify
them.
What centuries of inbreeding do produce is the concentration of every other deleterious
recessive trait in the family genome: instability, illness, madness, infertility. The oldest
pureblood houses are biologically the most damaged.
Muggle-borns get the magic-trait fresh from healthier genetic backgrounds. They are
not weaker than purebloods. They are, on average, stronger, because the rest of their biology
isn't compromised. The pureblood ideology isn't merely morally wrong. It is factually wrong.


The Limit of What Can Be Known From Inside
Any sufficiently curious magical scholar can work out the genetic mechanism. Magic is
inherited. It follows carrier-trait rules. Muggle-borns come from forgotten ancestry. Squibs are
dormant carriers.
What such a scholar cannot determine, from inside a library, is where the original trait came
from. The natural assumption is ancient mutation. The truth — that the trait came from the
First Ones, that every wizard is the distant descendant of a Vhor'keth bond — is invisible from
the outside, because no record of it survives in any wizarding text.
The genetics are knowable. The origin is not. That gap is where the lore lives.
VI. THE PRESENT STATE OF THE FIRST ONES
Survival
Most First Ones who survived the Fall live now as house-elves: bound by the Keth'vorr,
shaped continuously by the chains into the small, deferential form wizards recognize. The
chains hold. The pretense holds. They serve, they endure.
The vast majority of modern house-elves do not know what they are. The memory has been
deliberately diluted across generations, smothered under the conditioning of servitude. The
knowledge survives only in fragments, carried by a small number who have been entrusted
with it — usually elders, usually in oldest houses.
The Wild
A vanishing few First Ones exist in places humans have never penetrated — deep beneath
certain mountains, in the lightless trenches of the sea, in pockets of forest old enough to
remember when the world was still settling. They are unbound, but they are also alone, and
most are very old, and many are fading without anyone to anchor them. They are myth even
to other First Ones.


Ward Mechanics
First One magic operates on an older substrate than wizard spellcraft. Most wizarding wards
— even powerful ones — were designed without knowledge of the First Ones, and First One
movement passes through them as water passes through a sieve. This is why house-elves
can apparate into and out of places that defy ordinary wizards.
There are limits. Founder-era wards on the oldest sites — parts of certain ancient castles,
some Goblin vaults, certain pre-Roman ruins — were built with First One knowledge
embedded in them, and can detect First Ones if the alarms are properly attuned.
A bonded First One can sync their presence to their Vhor'keth's signature, allowing wards to
read them as an extension of the human.
What a Freed First One Looks Like
When the Keth'vorr breaks on a given individual, the body-binding falls away first and falls
away completely. The little servant straightens. Lengthens. The features sharpen, the eyes
shift, the skin recovers its faint translucence. The redirection collapses next, sometimes more
slowly, and the First One's full power begins to return as the channels widen.
The first wizards to witness a freeing will not understand what they are looking at. The
thing they thought of as a creature of their household will be revealed as a member of an older
people their civilization was built on the back of.
VII. THE OLD TONGUE
The language of the First Ones is older than any human speech. It has two layers, and only
one of them can be learned from a book.
The Surface Layer
The vocabulary and grammar can be learned from outside, like any language. A determined
human scholar with access to a First One willing to teach could build a working knowledge.
The vocabulary is large but finite. The grammar is unusual but mappable.


The Resonance Overlay
The deeper layer is the felt-state the speaker carries while speaking. Every word has a
required internal state that the speaker must actually be in, or the word means something
other than what its dictionary definition suggests.
A human speaking the Old Tongue while emotionally misaligned produces grammatically
correct sentences that mean different things than intended. Not nonsense — wrong
meaning. Vhor'ketha spoken without the felt-state of willing dissolution becomes a word
about taking; Vhor'keth spoken without the felt-state of reverence becomes a word about
possession. The errors are not random. They are the shadow versions of the meanings the
speaker thought they were producing.
This is recoverable. A speaker can learn to align their inner state to their words the way an
actor learns to access real emotion on cue. It is difficult. It is also possible.
Why No Human Has Spoken It Correctly in a Thousand Years
Not because it is metaphysically locked. Because it is sociologically locked. The Old Tongue
can be taught, but only by a First One willing to teach, and no First One has been willing to
teach since the Fall.
The relationship between teacher and student in the Old Tongue is itself part of the language
— you cannot learn the resonance overlay without being in the kind of relationship with a First
One that produces the right internal states in the first place.
Any human who eventually learns to speak the Old Tongue correctly will do so
because a specific First One chose them. The unlocking of the language will be a story
event, not a background possibility.

VIII. STRUCTURAL PRINCIPLES OF THE LORE
A few load-bearing ideas, stated plainly:
1. The First Ones are not gods. They are a dying people. Their power is real but finite, and
getting smaller every age. The world has moved on without them.
2. Bonding is survival before it is love. The First Mother did not bond for romance. She
bonded because the alternative was dissolution. Love is what grows in the soil of that need. It
is real, but it is not the foundation.
3. The bond has a coercive substrate. Mortal-radiance is not consensual to give. The First
One survives off the human regardless of the human's consent or affection. Every bond is, at
the biological level, an act the human cannot fully refuse once it has been begun. The First
Ones do not see this. That blindness is the original wound of the entire system.
4. The betrayal was internal. The First Ones were not conquered by an enemy. They were
enslaved by their own descendants, using knowledge that could only come from inside the


bond. The wizarding world's foundational crime is familial.
5. The chains exploit love, not power. The Keth'vorr does not suppress First One ability. It
uses the First One's love for their descendants as the lock. They could break free if they were
willing to hurt their own children. They are not.
6. Every witch and wizard is, eventually, a descendant of a Vhor'keth bond. The
pureblood/half-blood/Muggle-born distinction is meaningless at the level of origin. They are all
family. The chains hold them all in the same web.
7. The line is dormant, not closed. No new Vhor'kethi have been born in a thousand years
because the chains prevent the bond. Free a First One, and the bond becomes possible
again.
8. The truth is recoverable but not from inside the system. A scholar can map the
genetics. A First One can speak the history. But no purely wizarding investigation can arrive at
the origin without a witness from the older world.
IX. THE NOVEL OUTLINE
PART ONE: THE LONG AGES

- Aen-Vhar is a wanderer, curious in a way her people are not always curious
- She has noticed the world is changing — responsiveness is less than it used to be
- Ends with her sitting by a river that used to come to meet her and now does not

- They are bright — radiant, present, alive in a register her own people increasingly are not
